title: What to Bring on a 50k Trail Run: The Complete Packing List (2026)
excerpt: Planning your first or next 50k? Here is everything you need to bring on a 50k trail run, from hydration vests to drop bag essentials and mandatory gear.
body:
What to Bring on a 50k Trail Run: The Complete Race Day Packing List

You're standing at the start line before dawn, headlamp cutting through the dark, and the trail stretches somewhere ahead of you into the trees. The next 8 to 12 hours will test your legs, your head, and your gear. Knowing what to bring on a 50k trail run is not just about comfort. It is about finishing safely and running the race you trained for.

A 50k is roughly 31 miles, and it occupies a unique space in ultramarathon distances. It is long enough to punish underpacking but short enough that you do not need to haul a basecamp on your back. This guide walks through every gear category you need to consider, from hydration systems to drop bag strategy, so you can make smart decisions before race morning.

---

Hydration Gear: The Foundation of Your 50k Setup

Hydration is the single most important logistical decision you will make for a 50k. Most organized trail races no longer provide cups at aid stations, which means you need your own system every time you roll through. Most runners find that carrying two to four 500ml soft flasks covers the distance between aid stations on typical courses.

A trail running hydration pack or vest is the most practical choice for a 50k. It keeps your hands free, distributes weight evenly across your chest, and reduces the up-and-down bounce that kills your shoulders on longer efforts. Look for vests with at least 1.5 liters of total storage capacity and front flask pockets that are easy to access without stopping.

If your expected finish time is over eight hours, a bladder-style hydration system sitting behind your shoulders offers extra reserve capacity. Some runners with longer finish windows, such as those targeting 11 to 12 hours on technical terrain, find a dedicated hydration vest far more comfortable than handheld bottles alone over that duration. Choose the system that fits your pace, your course, and the distance between aid stations on your specific race.

---

50k Trail Running Gear: Clothing and Footwear

Your clothing choices matter more on a 50k than on a road race because conditions can shift dramatically over several hours on the trail. Start with a moisture-wicking base layer that moves sweat away from your skin without holding it. Over that, carry a lightweight windproof or waterproof jacket, even if the morning forecast looks clean.

A lightweight waterproof jacket is mandatory kit at most organized 50k events worldwide. Race directors require it because weather can turn without much warning, particularly at elevation. A jacket weighing under 150 grams, such as those in the Salomon Bonatti or Montane Minimus range, packs into a palm-sized bundle and adds almost nothing to your overall load. Do not leave it behind just because the sky looks blue.

Trail-specific shoes with adequate grip and protection matter more at 50k than at shorter distances because your feet absorb thousands more repetitive impacts on variable terrain. Match your shoe to the course. Rocky technical terrain calls for a rockplate and stiffer midsole. Looser, rooty singletrack may suit a more flexible shoe with aggressive lugs. Carry a spare pair of socks in your vest or drop bag, because wet feet over 31 miles increase blister risk significantly.

---

Trail Running Essentials: Safety and Emergency Gear

Remote trail terrain demands that you carry a basic safety kit. Most race organizations will list mandatory gear that must be on your person at all times during the event. Common mandatory items include a space blanket or emergency bivy, a first aid kit, a whistle, and a communication device such as a fully charged phone.

A headlamp is essential even if you plan to finish well before dark. Races with generous cutoff times mean some runners will finish in the evening or night hours. The Serpent Trail 50k, for example, notes that darkness arrives around 10pm on race day, which makes a headlamp necessary for any runner targeting a finish time beyond 11 hours. Carry spare batteries or a charged backup battery bank regardless of your expected finish window.

Gaiters are a small addition with a significant payoff on sandy, muddy, or loose terrain. They seal the opening between your shoe and your sock, keeping rocks, dirt, and debris from building up around your foot during the race. A gritty stone rubbing against your heel for 20 miles is a race ender. Short ankle gaiters from brands like Inov-8 or Dirty Girl add almost nothing to your kit weight and protect you from a very fixable problem.

---

Ultra Running Nutrition: Fueling for the Long Haul

Your body can store roughly 90 minutes of glycogen at race pace. Everything beyond that requires you to replace carbohydrates consistently and deliberately. Most ultramarathon nutrition strategies aim for 60 to 90 grams of carbohydrate per hour during sustained efforts, sourced from a mix of gels, chews, real food, and aid station offerings.

Variety matters on a 50k because flavor fatigue is real. If you rely on one gel brand for 30 miles, the thought of swallowing another packet around mile 22 can feel unbearable. Pack a mix of formats. Gels work well early when your stomach is fresh. Solid food like rice balls, boiled potatoes, or a small sandwich becomes more appealing later in the race when your gut wants something substantial.

Electrolytes deserve as much attention as calories. Sodium losses through sweat can contribute to cramping and fatigue over the course of a 50k, particularly in warm conditions. Salt tablets, electrolyte capsules, and sports drinks at aid stations all address this. Some runners find that staying ahead of sodium loss, rather than chasing it after symptoms appear, makes the final miles far more manageable. If you experience persistent cramping or muscle issues during long efforts, that is worth discussing with a sports dietitian or physiotherapist before your next race.

---

What to Pack in Your 50k Drop Bag

Not every 50k race allows drop bags, but when they do, use them strategically. A drop bag is your portable supply depot at a designated aid station. It is not a suitcase. Pack only what you will actually use.

Key 50k drop bag items include:

  • A spare pair of trail shoes and socks, in case your primary pair becomes soaked or damaged
  • An additional 500ml soft flask in case one leaks or fails
  • Extra race nutrition, specifically food types you know your stomach tolerates late in a race
  • A dry long-sleeve layer or arm warmers if temperatures are expected to drop in the afternoon
  • Anti-chafe products like Body Glide or petroleum jelly for areas that have started to rub
  • Blister care supplies including small scissors, needle, tape, and foam padding
  • A fully charged power bank for your phone or GPS watch
Label your drop bag clearly with your name and race number. Pack it in a logical order so you can grab what you need quickly and keep moving. The goal is to spend under three minutes at a drop bag station, not to sit down and reorganize your life.

---

Technology and Tracking Gear for Your 50k

A GPS watch is not mandatory, but it is one of the most useful tools you can wear on a 50k. It keeps you honest about pace, tracks elevation gain, and gives you a real-time sense of how much distance remains. Watches from Garmin, Suunto, and Coros all offer trail-specific modes with accurate elevation data and battery life that covers a full 50k with room to spare.

Pre-load the course GPX file onto your watch before race day. Even well-marked courses have sections where confidence in your direction matters. Having the route on your wrist reduces the mental load during the back half of the race when decision fatigue is real.

Some runners carry a handheld GPS device as a backup on remote or backcountry 50k courses. This is more common on unsupported or lightly supported events than on organized races with clear course marking. Match your technology choices to the specific demands of your event.

---

Race-Specific Mandatory Gear: Read the Fine Print

Every organized 50k has a mandatory gear list, and that list is not a suggestion. Race officials check kit before the start and sometimes conduct spot checks on the course. Showing up without a required item can mean a time penalty, a forced scratch, or being turned around at an aid station.

Download your specific race's mandatory gear list and check every item off physically the night before. Common mandatory kit requirements across 50k events include a waterproof jacket, space blanket, whistle, first aid kit, headlamp with spare batteries, a minimum hydration capacity, and a communication device. Some mountain and alpine 50k races add trekking poles, gloves, and a hat to the list depending on the terrain and time of year.

If pain or discomfort develops during the race, do not push through without assessing. Sharp joint pain, significant swelling, or pain that changes your gait are worth pausing for. If pain persists after the race or develops into a recurring issue during training, consult a doctor or physiotherapist before your next event.

---

Conclusion: Pack Smart, Run Light, Finish Strong

Knowing what to bring on a 50k trail run comes down to honest self-assessment. Understand your expected finish time, study your course terrain, check your mandatory gear list, and build your kit from there. You want to carry everything you need and nothing you do not.

The runners who struggle with gear on a 50k are usually those who either packed too heavy and wore themselves out lugging unnecessary weight, or those who went too minimal and found themselves cold, dehydrated, or underfueled in the final miles. The sweet spot is a well-considered vest with a reliable hydration system, a safety kit that meets mandatory requirements, race-tested nutrition, and a focused drop bag at your midpoint aid station.

Start your gear check two days before the race. Lay everything out. Weigh your vest once it is loaded. Then go run a confident, well-supported 50k.

---

keyTakeaway: Runners attempting their first 50K often underpack critical items or carry too much weight, so this guide provides a tested gear checklist organized by mandatory requirements, nutrition needs, and personal comfort items to ensure race day success without unnecessary burden.
